bibdata.title: OGC Emission Event Modeling Language (EmissionML) Part I: Data Model

abstract.p: This OGC Standard defines EmissionML, a conceptual data model for representing emission events. EmissionML provides a domain-specific ontology for describing the release of substances into the atmosphere, including the source from which an emission originates, the mechanism by which it occurs, the quantity of substance released, and the temporal bounds of the event.

EmissionML distinguishes itself through three key design principles. First, it incorporates an evidence chain linking emission determinations to supporting Observations, enabling traceability from reported values to underlying measurements. Second, it provides explicit uncertainty provenance by attaching data quality metadata to both emission quantities and temporal bounds, ensuring that the confidence and precision of each determination are recorded alongside the values themselves. Third, it offers clear spatial and temporal context — spatial through Feature (as the ultimate feature of interest) and Source (as the emission origin), temporal through start time, end time, and the phenomenon time of supporting Observations. By adopting the OMS 3.0 Observation model, EmissionML provides a common framework for reconciling heterogeneous sensor observations — from remote sensing satellites to ground-based continuous monitoring stations — within a single, unified evidence chain.

The model builds on foundational OGC and ISO standards. Source inherits from Feature (ISO 19109), DeterminationProcedure inherits from Procedure (ISO/OGC 19156:2023), and Observations conform to the OMS 3.0 Observation model (ISO/OGC 19156:2023). The standard defines twelve requirements specifying the structure and constraints of the core data model.

By making emission semantics, evidence chains, and uncertainty provenance explicit and machine-verifiable, EmissionML enables credibility of reported emission values, traceability from determinations to their supporting observations and procedures, and reproducibility of emission assessments across organizations and jurisdictions.

The intended audience includes environmental regulators, greenhouse gas reporting frameworks, emissions monitoring system developers, and geospatial data infrastructure implementers who require a standardized, interoperable representation of emission event data.

clause.title: Copyright notice
clause.p:
Copyright © 2026 Open Geospatial Consortium
To obtain additional rights of use, visit

clause.title: Note
clause.p: Attention is drawn to the possibility that some of the elements of this document may be the subject of patent rights. The Open Geospatial Consortium shall not be held responsible for identifying any or all such patent rights.

Recipients of this document are requested to submit, with their comments, notification of any relevant patent claims or other intellectual property rights of which they may be aware that might be infringed by any implementation of the standard set forth in this document, and to provide supporting documentation.

clause.title: License Agreement
clause.p: Use of this document is subject to the license agreement at

clause.title: Notice
clause.p: This document is not an OGC Standard. This document is an OGC Draft Standard and is therefore not an official position of the OGC membership. It is distributed for review and comment. It is subject to change without notice and may not be referred to as an OGC Standard.
Further, an OGC Draft Standard should not be referenced as required or mandatory technology in procurements.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: This OGC Standard defines EmissionML, a conceptual data model for representing emission events. EmissionML provides a domain-specific ontology for describing the release of substances into the atmosphere, including the source from which an emission originates, the mechanism by which it occurs, the quantity of substance released, and the temporal bounds of the event.

EmissionML distinguishes itself through three key design principles. First, it incorporates an evidence chain linking emission determinations to supporting Observations, enabling traceability from reported values to underlying measurements. Second, it provides explicit uncertainty provenance by attaching data quality metadata to both emission quantities and temporal bounds, ensuring that the confidence and precision of each determination are recorded alongside the values themselves. Third, it offers clear spatial and temporal context — spatial through Feature (as the ultimate feature of interest) and Source (as the emission origin), temporal through start time, end time, and the phenomenon time of supporting Observations. By adopting the OMS 3.0 Observation model, EmissionML provides a common framework for reconciling heterogeneous sensor observations — from remote sensing satellites to ground-based continuous monitoring stations — within a single, unified evidence chain.

The model builds on foundational OGC and ISO standards. Source inherits from Feature (ISO 19109), DeterminationProcedure inherits from Procedure (ISO/OGC 19156:2023), and Observations conform to the OMS 3.0 Observation model (ISO/OGC 19156:2023). The standard defines twelve requirements specifying the structure and constraints of the core data model.

By making emission semantics, evidence chains, and uncertainty provenance explicit and machine-verifiable, EmissionML enables credibility of reported emission values, traceability from determinations to their supporting observations and procedures, and reproducibility of emission assessments across organizations and jurisdictions.

The intended audience includes environmental regulators, greenhouse gas reporting frameworks, emissions monitoring system developers, and geospatial data infrastructure implementers who require a standardized, interoperable representation of emission event data.

note.p: Insert Preface Text here. Give OGC specific commentary: describe the technical content, reason for document, history of the document and precursors, and plans for future work. > Attention is drawn to the possibility that some of the elements of this document may be the subject of patent rights. The Open Geospatial Consortium shall not be held responsible for identifying any or all such patent rights.

Recipients of this document are requested to submit, with their comments, notification of any relevant patent claims or other intellectual property rights of which they may be aware that might be infringed by any implementation of the standard set forth in this document, and to provide supporting documentation.

clause.title: Security Considerations
clause.p: No security considerations have been made for this Standard.

clause.title: Submitters
clause.p: All questions regarding this submission should be directed to the editor or the submitters:

clause.title: Keywords
clause.p: The following are keywords to be used by search engines and document catalogues.

ogcdoc, OGC document, EmissionML, emission event, atmospheric emissions, greenhouse gas, air quality, emission source, emission quantity, observation, data quality, uncertainty provenance, evidence chain, interoperability, net-zero, methane emissions, sensor web

clause.title: Submitting organizations
clause.p: The following organizations submitted this Document to the Open Geospatial Consortium (OGC):

li.p: SensorUp Inc.
Natural Resources Canada
University of Calgary

clause.title: Scope
clause.p: This document defines a conceptual data model for emission events, for features from which emissions originate and mechanisms by which they occur, and for observations and procedures that provide evidence for emission determinations. These provide models for the exchange of information describing emission events and their associated quantities, temporal bounds, sources, and mechanisms, both within and between different regulatory, scientific, and technical communities.

Emission events commonly involve observations of an ultimate feature-of-interest at varying spatial scales. This document defines a common set of classes for describing emission sources, mechanisms, quantities, temporal bounds, determination procedures, and supporting observations. The schema includes relationships between emission events and their evidence chains, linking reported values to underlying observations and procedures, and attaches data quality metadata to emission quantities and temporal bounds.

This document concerns only externally visible interfaces and places no restriction on the underlying implementations other than what is needed to satisfy the interface specifications in the actual situation.

clause.title: Conformance
clause.p: This Standard defines a conceptual data model for emission events.
Requirements for one standardization target type are considered:

li.p: Data Model

clause.p: Conformance with this Standard shall be checked using all the relevant tests specified in Annex A (normative) of this document. The framework, concepts, and methodology for testing, and the criteria to be achieved to claim conformance are specified in the OGC Compliance Testing Policies and Procedures and the OGC Compliance Testing web site.
In order to conform to this OGC® Standard, a software implementation shall choose to implement:

li.p: The Emission Event Conceptual Data Model Core conformance class specified in Annex A (normative).

clause.p: All requirements-classes and conformance-classes described in this document are owned by the Standard(s) identified.

terms.title: Terms and definitions
terms.p: No terms and definitions are listed in this document.

This document uses the terms defined in OGC Policy Directive 49, which is based on the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2, Rules for the structure and drafting of International Standards. In particular, the word “shall” (not “must”) is the verb form used to indicate a requirement to be strictly followed to conform to this Standard and OGC documents do not use the equivalent phrases in the ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2.

This document also uses terms defined in the OGC Standard for Modular specifications (OGC 08-131r3), also known as the ‘ModSpec’. The definitions of terms such as standard, specification, requirement, and conformance test are provided in the ModSpec.
For the purposes of this document, the following additional terms and definitions apply.

dd.p: A Determination Procedure describes the steps performed to determine the quantity or temporal bounds of an Emission Event.

An Emission Event represents the release of a substance into the atmosphere over a non-zero duration. It is characterized by its start time and end time, the Source Feature from which the substance originated, and the identity and quantity of the emitted substance.
An Emission Quantity is the physical quantity representing the total mass of substance released to the atmosphere during a defined Emission Event or over a specified time interval.
Emission Intent is a code list describing whether an emission is intentional, unintentional, or unknown.
Abstraction of real world phenomena (Source: ISO 19101-1:2014)
Set of operations having the object of determining the value of a quantity (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023)

A Mechanism is a classification of the specific physical, chemical, or operational process that constitutes the direct cause or pathway for an emission. It is defined independently of the Source—including its identity, structure, or purpose—allowing Mechanisms to be consistently reused across different types of Source.

Act carried out by an observer to determine the value of an observable property of an object (feature-of-interest) by using a procedure, with the value provided as the result (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023)

A Source represents the feature from which an emission originates. A Source is an ISO 191xx feature whose geometry provides the geospatial reference for one or more Emission Events. A Source is characterised by its own identity, geometry, intended function, and lifecycle, and is explicitly agnostic to the Mechanism by which any associated Emission Event occurs.
A Source Type classifies a Source by its category.
A Temporal Bound is a value object representing a temporal determination (e.g., start time or end time) that MAY have associated data quality metadata.
Reference quantity chosen from a unit equivalence group (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023)

clause.title: Overview
clause.p: This section provides details and examples for any conventions used in the document. Examples of conventions are symbols, abbreviations, use of XML schema, or special notes regarding how to read the document.

clause.title: Identifiers
clause.p: The normative provisions in this Standard are denoted by the URI
All requirements and conformance tests that appear in this document are denoted by partial URIs which are relative to this base.

clause.title: Overview
clause.p: Emissions measurement, monitoring, reporting, and verification (MMRV) now spans satellite remote sensing, aerial surveys, continuous in-situ monitoring, and handheld inspection instruments. Each modality generates data at different spatial and temporal scales, yet regulators, operators, and verifiers must reconcile these heterogeneous observations into coherent, auditable emission assessments. The absence of a common ontology and data model for emission events is the primary structural barrier to achieving this reconciliation at scale.

EmissionML addresses this gap. It defines a standards-grounded conceptual model that provides a consistent semantic foundation for describing emission sources, events, quantities, and supporting observations — independent of the sensing technology, reporting framework, or enterprise system that produces or consumes the data.

clause.title: Structural Gaps in Current Approaches
clause.p: The emissions data ecosystem lacks a shared ontology. Each technology vendor, facility operator, and reporting program defines its own vocabulary, formats, and assumptions. This fragmentation produces systemic inefficiencies across the entire data lifecycle:

li.p: Costly, manual, and siloed workflows. Operators must build bespoke integrations to reconcile proprietary vendor outputs with internal enterprise systems, duplicating effort for each new sensor or data source.

Inability to link heterogeneous measurements. A satellite-detected concentration anomaly and a ground-level inspection of the same source cannot be programmatically correlated when source features, event semantics, and observation metadata are defined inconsistently.

Incompatible reporting requirements. The same emission data must be restated in multiple incompatible templates to satisfy different regulatory and voluntary frameworks (e.g., EPA, EU Methane Regulation, OGMP 2.0, MiQ), increasing administrative burden and undermining cross-framework comparability.

Lack of transparency and auditability. Without a standard evidence chain linking reported quantities to their supporting observations and determination procedures, stakeholders cannot independently verify the credibility of emission assessments.

clause.p: These are not incidental inefficiencies. They are consequences of a missing data foundation: no common model exists for the emission event itself — the central entity that connects a source, a mechanism, a quantity, a time interval, and the observations that support the determination.

clause.title: How EmissionML Addresses These Gaps
clause.p: EmissionML operationalizes open standards to provide a sensor-agnostic, interoperable data model for emission events. It establishes:

li.p: A shared ontology for emission events and their constituent parts — Source, Mechanism, Emission Quantity, Temporal Bound, Emission Intent, and Determination Procedure — each with well-defined semantics and relationships.

An explicit evidence chain linking every emission determination to its supporting Observations (ISO/OGC 19156:2023, OMS 3.0) and procedures, enabling end-to-end traceability from reported values to underlying measurements.

Embedded uncertainty provenance through data quality metadata attached to emission quantities and temporal bounds, so that the confidence and precision of each determination are recorded alongside the values themselves.

A “write once, use many times” intermediary model that decouples data production from reporting consumption, enabling a single standards-conformant emission event record to be transformed into outputs for multiple regulatory and voluntary frameworks.

clause.p: illustrates this architecture. Heterogeneous inputs — emission inventories, sensor observations, operational data, geospatial data, and field inspection and repair data — are normalized through EmissionML into a single standards-conformant representation. From that representation, outputs are generated for multiple downstream consumers without requiring source-specific reformatting.

figure.name: EmissionML as an intermediary data model: write once, use many times

clause.p: EmissionML does not replace existing sensing technologies, estimation methods, or reporting tools. It provides the standards-grounded data foundation that enables them to interoperate.

clause.title: Applicability Across Emission Domains
clause.p: Although early development of EmissionML has been informed by oil and gas methane monitoring — where regulatory pressure and sensing technology are most advanced — the conceptual model is domain-neutral. The core abstraction of an emission event (a source releasing a substance through a mechanism, over a time interval, with a determined quantity) applies wherever substances are released into the atmosphere. Examples include:

li.p: Emergency management. Wildfires, industrial accidents, and volcanic events produce large, episodic emissions whose source geometry, temporal bounds, and quantities must be characterized rapidly from heterogeneous observations (satellite imagery, ground sensors, dispersion models) and communicated across jurisdictions.

Landfill gas emissions. Landfills emit methane and other gases through diffuse and point sources that vary with waste composition, cover integrity, and weather conditions. Standardized emission event records enable consistent monitoring and reporting across sites and regulatory regimes.

Permafrost thaw. Thawing permafrost releases methane and carbon dioxide through mechanisms that are spatially diffuse and temporally variable. Reconciling field measurements, remote sensing, and biogeochemical models requires a common event model with explicit uncertainty provenance.

Agriculture. Livestock, rice cultivation, and fertilizer application produce emissions through distinct mechanisms at varying spatial scales. A shared ontology enables aggregation from field-level observations to regional inventories.

Wastewater treatment. Treatment processes emit methane, nitrous oxide, and other gases through identifiable mechanisms at known facilities, requiring the same source–event–observation linkage that EmissionML provides.

Public safety and hazardous releases. Chemical spills, industrial gas leaks, and radiological dispersal events (e.g., dirty bombs) require rapid characterization of what substance was released, from where, in what quantity, and over what time interval — the same source–mechanism–quantity–temporal structure that EmissionML defines. A standardized event model enables interoperable data exchange across first responders, environmental agencies, and public health authorities.

clause.p: The EmissionML conceptual model accommodates these domains without modification to the core classes or relationships. Domain-specific extensions — such as specialized Source Type or Mechanism vocabularies — can be defined as profiles of the core model.

clause.title: Use Cases
clause.p: summarizes the key use cases that motivate the design of EmissionML.

tr.td: Proprietary payloads and inconsistent data models require bespoke integrations for each sensor system, increasing cost and preventing plug-and-play deployment.
Normalizes observation payloads to a consistent ontology, eliminating vendor-specific integration overhead.

Satellite-detected concentration anomalies cannot be programmatically linked to specific sources (e.g., facilities, landfill cells, wildfire perimeters) when source feature definitions are inconsistent across systems.
Links Observations to Source Features and Emission Events with an auditable validation chain, reducing attribution uncertainty.
Operators reformat the same emission data for each reporting framework (EPA, EU Methane Regulation, OGMP 2.0, MiQ), duplicating effort and introducing transcription errors.
Provides a single standards-conformant emission event record that can be automatically transformed into multiple reporting formats.
Control rooms and emergency response centres receive fragmented event feeds from multiple systems, delaying root-cause diagnosis and coordinated action.
Delivers a normalized, brokerable event stream with consistent source and quantity semantics across platforms.
Emission reduction calculations are opaque, with no standard mechanism to encode uncertainties or link quantities to their determination procedures.
Encodes emission quantities with uncertainties and provenance metadata, increasing credit integrity and reducing verification cost.
Simulation tools use incompatible models, limiting cross-study comparability and reuse of inputs and outputs.
Represents simulation inputs and outputs as standardized Emission Events, enabling benchmarking and reproducibility.
ESG risk assessments depend on inconsistent, non-comparable emissions disclosures that cannot be reliably ingested into financial risk models.
Structures emissions data with standardized semantics and uncertainty metadata for direct ingestion into risk and ESG scoring models.

clause.title: Further Reading
clause.p: The rationale, use cases, and design principles summarized in this clause are discussed in detail in the OGC EmissionML Discussion Paper .

clause.title: Overview
clause.p: The EmissionML conceptual model is described as a class diagram in . The schema is fully described in x.x.x.

table.name: UML relationship notation used in this standard

tr.td: The target is owned by and cannot exist without the source (e.g., EmissionEvent owns its EmissionQuantity and TemporalBound instances)
A directional reference between independent classes (e.g., EmissionEvent refers to a Source)

clause.p: Use the following table for Requirements Classes.

table.name: Requirements class — Emission Event Conceptual Data Model Core

td.strong: Emission Event Conceptual Data Model Core

tr.td: This requirement defines the Emission Event.
This requirement defines the Source Type.
This requirement defines the Emission Quantity.
This requirement defines the Unit of Measure.
This requirement defines the Temporal Bound.
This requirement defines the Emission Intent.
This requirement defines the Determination Procedure.

clause.title: Emission Event Class
clause.p:
An Emission Event represents the release of a substance into the atmosphere over a non-zero duration. It is characterized by its start time and end time, the Source Feature from which the substance originated, and the identity and quantity of the emitted substance.
Conceptually, an Emission Event corresponds to an occurrent in upper-ontology terms, as it unfolds over time and has temporal parts.
For reporting or accounting purposes, a long-running emission event is often segmented into multiple time-bounded emission events (e.g., annual records). This segmentation reflects reporting conventions and does not imply physical cessation or re-initiation of the emission.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/emission-event
Each Emission Event class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in . Each Emission Event class shall have the direct relation between an Emission Event class and other classes listed in .

table.name: Attributes of an Emission Event class

tr.td: The URI linking to an external definition of the pollutantType of the Emission Event.

table.name: Direct relation between an Emission Event class and other class types

tr.td: The mechanism by which the emission occurred.
The source from which the emission originated.
The start time of the emission event.
The end time of the emission event.
The quantity of the substance emitted.
The procedure used to determine the start time.
The procedure used to determine the end time.
The procedure used to determine the emission quantity.
Observations providing evidence for the start time determination.
Observations providing evidence for the end time determination.
Observations providing evidence for the quantity determination.

clause.title: Feature Class
clause.p: A Feature is an abstraction of real world phenomena (Source: ISO 19101-1:2014). In EmissionML, Feature serves as the base class for entities that can be the ultimate feature of interest of an Observation. A Feature’s geometry attribute provides the geospatial reference for the phenomenon it represents.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/feature
+ Each Feature class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in .
The spatial geometry of the feature.

table.name: Direct relation between a Feature class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for Feature in the core model (it is referenced by Source via inheritance and by Observation via ultimateFeatureOfInterest).

clause.title: Source Class
clause.p:
A Source represents the feature from which an emission originates. A Source is an ISO 191xx feature whose geometry provides the geospatial reference for one or more Emission Events. A Source is characterised by its own identity, geometry, intended function, and lifecycle, and is explicitly agnostic to the Mechanism by which any associated Emission Event occurs.
A Source is a continuant that may exist independently of any emission, whereas an Emission Event is an occurrent. The identity and lifecycle of a Source are independent of the existence, absence, or frequency of Emission Events.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/source
+ Each Source class shall inherit from Feature. Each Source class shall have the optional attributes listed in . Each Source class shall have the direct relation between a Source class and other classes listed in .

table.name: Attributes of a Source class (own attributes; geometry is inherited from Feature)

tr.td: A human-readable name for the source.

table.name: Direct relation between a Source class and other class types

tr.td: The type classification of the source.

clause.title: Source Type Class
clause.p: A Source Type classifies a Source by its category.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/source-type
+ Each Source Type class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in .

table.name: Attributes of a Source Type class

tr.td: A human-readable label for the source type.
A detailed description of the source type.
The URI linking to an external definition of the source type.

table.name: Direct relation between a Source Type class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for SourceType in the core model (it is referenced by Source).

clause.title: Mechanism Class
clause.p: A Mechanism is a classification of the specific physical, chemical, or operational process that constitutes the direct cause or pathway for an emission. It is defined independently of the Source—including its identity, structure, or purpose—allowing Mechanisms to be consistently reused across different types of Source.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/mechanism
+ Each Mechanism class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in . Each Mechanism class shall have the direct relation listed in .
A human-readable label for the mechanism.
A detailed description of the mechanism.

table.name: Direct relation between a Mechanism class and other class types

tr.td: The emission intent associated with the mechanism.

clause.title: Emission Quantity Class
clause.p: An Emission Quantity is the physical quantity representing the total mass of substance released to the atmosphere during a defined Emission Event or over a specified time interval.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/emission-quantity
+ Each Emission Quantity class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in . Each Emission Quantity class shall have the direct relation between an Emission Quantity class and other classes listed in .

table.name: Attributes of an Emission Quantity class

tr.td: The numeric value of the emission quantity.
A data quality element describing the quality of the emission quantity value.

table.name: Direct relation between an Emission Quantity class and other class types

tr.td: The unit of measure associated with the emission quantity.

clause.title: Unit of Measure Class
clause.p: A Unit of Measure is a reference quantity chosen from a unit equivalence group (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023)

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/unit-of-measure
+ Each Unit of Measure class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in .

table.name: Attributes of a Unit of Measure class

tr.td: A human-readable label for the unit of measure.
A short symbol representing the unit of measure.
The URI linking to an external definition of the unit of measure.

table.name: Direct relation between a Unit of Measure class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for UnitOfMeasure in the core model.

clause.title: Temporal Bound Class
clause.p: A Temporal Bound is a value object representing a temporal determination (e.g., start time or end time) that MAY have associated data quality metadata. The determination procedure and supporting evidence for a Temporal Bound are tracked at the Emission Event level.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/temporal-bound
+ Each Temporal Bound class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in .

table.name: Attributes of a Temporal Bound class

tr.td: The time instant value of this Temporal Bound.
A data quality element describing the quality of the temporal bound.

table.name: Direct relation between a Temporal Bound class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for TemporalBound in the core model (determination procedure and evidence are tracked at the EmissionEvent level).

clause.title: Observation Class
clause.p:
An Observation is an act carried out by an observer to determine the value of an observable property of an object (feature-of-interest) by using a procedure, with the value provided as the result (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023). In EmissionML, an Observation is evidence that may support an Emission Quantity or Temporal Bound determination.
The Ultimate Feature of Interest of an Observation identifies a Feature — which may be a Source or another feature at a different spatial scale — associated with the Emission Event. It is common for a single Emission Event to be observed by multiple Observations whose Ultimate Features of Interest are defined at different spatial scales. For example, a satellite remote sensing image Observation typically has an area-based Ultimate Feature of Interest (e.g., a facility area or region), whereas the same Emission Event may also be observed by a person carrying an Optical Gas Imaging camera, for which the Ultimate Feature of Interest is a specific Source such as a component of an item of equipment from which pollutants are released.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/observation
+ Each Observation class shall be an ISO/OGC 19156:2023 (OGC OMS 3.0) Observation.

clause.title: Emission Intent Enumeration
clause.p: Emission Intent is a code list describing whether an emission is intentional, unintentional, or unknown.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/emission-intent
+ The Emission Intent enumeration shall support the enumeration values listed in .
The intent of the emission is unknown.

clause.title: Procedure Class
clause.p: A Procedure is a description of steps performed (Source: ISO/OGC 19156:2023). In EmissionML, Procedure serves as the base class for process descriptions such as DeterminationProcedure.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/procedure
+ Each Procedure class shall be an ISO/OGC 19156:2023 (OGC OMS 3.0) Procedure.
No own attributes are defined for Procedure in the core model (see the standard for complete specification).

table.name: Direct relation between a Procedure class and other class types

tr.td: No direct relations are defined for Procedure in the core model (it is referenced by DeterminationProcedure via inheritance).

clause.title: Determination Procedure Class
clause.p: A Determination Procedure describes the steps performed to determine the quantity or temporal bounds of an Emission Event. DeterminationProcedure inherits from Procedure, aligning it with the OMS 3.0 process model.

tr.td:
/req/datamodel/core/determination-procedure
+ Each Determination Procedure class shall inherit from Procedure. Each Determination Procedure class shall have the mandatory attributes listed in .

table.name: Attributes of a Determination Procedure class (own attributes)

tr.td: A human-readable label for the determination procedure.
A detailed description of the determination procedure.
The URI linking to an external definition of the determination procedure.

clause.title: Abstract tests for Emission Event Conceptual Data Model Core
Emission Event Conceptual Data Model Core

table.name: Conformance test — Emission Event Conceptual Data Model Core

tr.td: Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.
Verify that all requirements from the requirements class have been fulfilled.
Inspect the documentation of the application, schema or profile.

annex.title: Relationship with OGC Features and Observations

clause.title: Overview
clause.p: EmissionML is a domain-specific conceptual model that builds on two foundational OGC concepts: Features (ISO 19109 General Feature Model) and Observations (ISO/OGC 19156:2023, OMS 3.0). This annex explains how EmissionML relates to each and why explicit domain modeling is preferred over generic usage.
illustrates the conceptual layering of EmissionML on top of these foundational standards.

figure.name: Conceptual layering of EmissionML on foundational ISO/OGC standards

clause.title: EmissionML and OGC Features
clause.p: The OGC General Feature Model (ISO 19109) defines a Feature as an abstraction of a real-world phenomenon — any entity with an identity, properties, and optionally a geometry. EmissionML makes this alignment explicit by introducing a concrete Feature class (stereotyped <<ISO 19109>>) into the conceptual model.

The Feature class carries a mandatory geometry attribute of type GM_Object and serves as the base class for domain-specific feature types. Source inherits from Feature, gaining its geometry by inheritance. This structural inheritance — rather than a notional stereotype — makes the ISO 19109 alignment unambiguous and machine-verifiable.

Because Feature is concrete, an Observation’s ultimateFeatureOfInterest can reference any Feature — not only a Source. This enables observations whose feature of interest is defined at a different spatial scale (e.g., a facility area, a region, or a monitoring zone) to participate in the evidence chain without being forced into the Source class.

EmissionEvent is also a real-world phenomenon — an occurrent that unfolds over time. In principle, an Emission Event could be modeled as a generic Feature. However, EmissionML gives it explicit structure: temporal bounds (start time, end time), a quantity of emitted substance, a mechanism describing the physical or operational process, an intent classification, and an evidence chain linking the event to supporting Observations.

Without this explicit modeling, a Source would be indistinguishable from any other Feature, and an emission event would be “just another Feature” with ad-hoc properties. The semantic relationships that give emission data its meaning — hasSource, hasMechanism, hasQuantity, hasStartTimeEvidence — would be lost or require convention-based interpretation.

clause.title: EmissionML and OMS 3.0 Observations
clause.p: OMS 3.0 (ISO/OGC 19156:2023) defines an Observation as an act of determining the value of an observable property. An Observation has a well-defined structure: a feature of interest, an observed property, a procedure, and a result.

EmissionML incorporates OMS 3.0 Observation by reference (Requirement 8) — it does not redefine or extend the Observation class. In EmissionML, Observations serve as evidence supporting emission quantity and temporal bound determinations, linked through the hasQuantityEvidence, hasStartTimeEvidence, and hasEndTimeEvidence associations.

One might ask: Why not model the entire emission event as an Observation result? Because an Emission Event is richer than what a single Observation captures. An Emission Event has a source, a mechanism, an intent, multiple temporal bounds, and can be supported by multiple Observations at different spatial and temporal scales. For example:

li.p: A satellite remote sensing Observation may detect a methane plume over a broad area.
An Optical Gas Imaging (OGI) camera Observation may pinpoint the leak to a specific component.
A continuous emissions monitoring system (CEMS) Observation may quantify the emission rate over time.

clause.p: All three Observations can serve as evidence for a single Emission Event, each contributing to the determination of quantity, start time, or end time. The Observation’s ultimateFeatureOfInterest references a Feature — which may be a Source or any other Feature at a different spatial scale. The satellite Observation’s feature of interest may be a Feature representing an entire facility, while the OGI Observation’s feature of interest is a Source representing a single valve.

clause.title: EmissionML and OMS 3.0 Procedures
clause.p: OMS 3.0 defines a Procedure as “a description of steps performed” and introduces ObservingProcedure as a specialization that describes steps performed to determine the value of an observable property. EmissionML follows this same specialization pattern.

The EmissionML conceptual model introduces a Procedure class (stereotyped <<ISO 19156:2023>>) as the base class for process descriptions. DeterminationProcedure inherits from Procedure and describes the steps performed to determine a value — such as an emission quantity or temporal bound. This structural inheritance mirrors how ObservingProcedure specializes Procedure in OMS 3.0.

By making Procedure an explicit base class rather than a notional reference, EmissionML ensures that its process descriptions are structurally aligned with OMS 3.0 — not merely by naming convention, but by class hierarchy. This parallels the Feature/Source pattern: just as Source inherits from Feature to make ISO 19109 alignment concrete, DeterminationProcedure inherits from Procedure to make OMS 3.0 alignment concrete.

clause.title: Why Explicit Domain Modeling
clause.p: The alternative. One could model everything with generic Features and Observations — Sources as plain Features, emission data as Observation results, and relationships as ad-hoc properties. This approach uses only existing OGC standards without defining new classes.
The problem. Generic modeling loses domain semantics. Without EmissionML’s explicit classes, systems cannot:

li.p: distinguish a Source from any other Feature,
enforce that an emission has a Mechanism and an Intent,
trace the evidence chain from Observation to Emission Quantity to Emission Event,
validate that required relationships are present (e.g., every Emission Event must have a Source, a Mechanism, and a Determination Procedure).

dd.p: Systems exchanging EmissionML data share a common vocabulary. EmissionEvent, Source, Mechanism, and EmissionQuantity are unambiguous — they mean the same thing in every implementation.

Requirements can be formally specified and enforced. For example, Requirement 1 mandates that every Emission Event has a Source, a Mechanism, start time, quantity, and their corresponding Determination Procedures.

Domain experts work with emission concepts directly — emission events, sources, mechanisms — rather than mapping to and from generic abstractions. This reduces the cognitive burden and the risk of misinterpretation.

clause.p: Analogy. Just as CityGML defines explicit Building, Road, and Vegetation classes rather than using generic Features, EmissionML defines explicit emission domain classes. The General Feature Model provides the abstract foundation; EmissionML provides the domain-specific vocabulary that makes emission data interoperable and actionable.

references.title: Normative references
references.p: The following documents are referred to in the text in such a way that some or all of their content constitutes requirements of this document. For dated references, only the edition cited applies. For undated references, the latest edition of the referenced document (including any amendments) applies.

bibitem.title: Geographic information
Reference model
Part 1: Fundamentals
Geographic information — Reference model — Part 1: Fundamentals
bibitem.abstract: ISO 19101-1:2014 defines the reference model for standardization in the field of geographic information. This reference model describes the notion of interoperability and sets forth the fundamentals by which this standardization takes place. Although structured in the context of information technology and information technology standards, ISO 19101-1:2014 is independent of any application development method or technology implementation approach.

bibitem.title: Geographic information
Spatial schema
Geographic information — Spatial schema
bibitem.abstract: This document specifies conceptual schemas for describing the spatial characteristics of geographic entities, and a set of spatial operations consistent with these schemas. It treats “vector” geometry and topology. It defines standard spatial operations for use in access, query, management, processing and data exchange of geographic information for spatial (geometric and topological) objects. Because of the nature of geographic information, these geometric coordinate spaces will normally have up to three spatial dimensions, one temporal dimension and any number of other spatially dependent parameters as needed by the applications. In general, the topological dimension of the spatial projections of the geometric objects will be at most three.

bibitem.title: Geographic information
Temporal schema
Geographic information — Temporal schema
bibitem.abstract: ISO 19108:2002 defines concepts for describing temporal characteristics of geographic information. It depends upon existing information technology standards for the interchange of temporal information. It provides a basis for defining temporal feature attributes, feature operations, and feature associations, and for defining the temporal aspects of metadata about geographic information. Since this International Standard is concerned with the temporal characteristics of geographic information as they are abstracted from the real world, it emphasizes valid time rather than transaction time.

bibitem.title: Geographic information
Rules for application schema
Geographic information — Rules for application schema
Geographic information
Data quality
Geographic information — Data quality
Geographic information
Observations, measurements and samples
Geographic information — Observations, measurements and samples
bibitem.abstract: This document defines a conceptual schema for observations, for features involved in the observation process, and for features involved in sampling when making observations. These provide models for the exchange of information describing observation acts and their results, both within and between different scientific and technical communities. Observations commonly involve sampling of an ultimate feature-of-interest. This document defines a common set of sample types according to their spatial, material (for ex situ observations) or statistical nature. The schema includes relationships between sample features (sub-sampling, derived samples). This document concerns only externally visible interfaces and places no restriction on the underlying implementations other than what is needed to satisfy the interface specifications in the actual situation.

bibitem.title: Topic 20
Observations, measurements and samples
Topic 20 — Observations, measurements and samples
bibitem.abstract:
This document defines a conceptual schema for observations, for features involved in the observation process, and for features involved in sampling when making observations. These provide models for the exchange of information describing observation acts and their results, both within and between different scientific and technical communities.
Observations commonly involve sampling of an ultimate feature-of-interest. This document defines a common set of sample types according to their spatial, material (for ex situ observations) or statistical nature. The schema includes relationships between sample features (sub-sampling, derived samples).
This document concerns only externally visible interfaces and places no restriction on the underlying implementations other than what is needed to satisfy the interface specifications in the actual situation.

bibitem.formattedref: OGC: Introduction to OGC EmissionML: Why the World Needs a Standard Ontology for Emissions. OGC Discussion Paper (2025).
